sn9.1:1.2: that on one occasion a certain monk was staying among the Kosalans in a forest grove.
sn9.1:1.3: Now at that time, as he had gone to spend the day (in the grove), he was thinking unskillful thoughts, connected with the household life.
sn9.1:1.4: Then the devatā inhabiting the forest grove, feeling sympathy for the monk, desiring his benefit, desiring to bring him to his senses, approached him and addressed him with this verse:
sn9.1:2.2: and yet your mind goes running outside.
sn9.1:2.3: You, a person: subdue your desire for people.
sn9.1:2.4: Then you’ll be happy, free from passion.
sn9.1:3.2: Let me remind you of that which is good—
sn9.1:3.3: for the dust of the regions below is hard to transcend.
sn9.1:3.4: Don’t let the dust of the sensual pull you down.
sn9.1:4.1: As a bird spattered with dirt sheds
sn9.1:4.2: the adhering dust with a shake,
sn9.1:5.1: The monk, chastened by the devatā, came to his senses.
